title: India expands censorship powers, lets lower officials demand takedowns
summary_en: India launched the Sahyog platform in October 2024, extending content removal authority from two federal ministries to all government agencies and district-level officials. Legal experts argue this circumvents Supreme Court safeguards by using Section 79 of the IT Act instead of the previously regulated Section 69A, enabling widespread censorship with minimal oversight.

title: 'Deeply concerned' over India press censorship, says X as accounts blocked
summary_en: X announced it is 'deeply concerned about ongoing press censorship in India' after the Indian government ordered the platform to block over 2,300 accounts under Section 69A of the IT Act, including two Reuters news handles. The company restored the Reuters accounts following public outcry but reported the government demanded immediate compliance without justification.

title: X accuses India of press censorship after it blocks news outlets' accounts
summary_en: X accused the Indian government of press censorship after temporarily blocking accounts of international news organizations including Reuters, TRT World, and Global Times in early July 2025. CPJ criticized India's lack of transparency in social media enforcement, calling for clearer legal grounds and independent judicial oversight of such blocking orders.

title: India targeted high-profile journalists with Pegasus spyware: Amnesty
summary_en: An investigation by Amnesty International and The Washington Post revealed that India's government repeatedly used Pegasus spyware to target prominent journalists, including Siddharth Varadarajan who was targeted again on 16 October 2023. The findings documented surveillance of reporters working on sensitive stories, raising concerns about press freedom in the world's largest democracy.

title: Cloudflare's VPN app among half-dozen pulled from Indian app stores
summary_en: More than half-a-dozen VPN apps including Cloudflare's 1.1.1.1, Hide.me, and PrivadoVPN were pulled from India's Apple App Store and Google Play Store following orders from the Ministry of Home Affairs. The enforcement marks the first major use of India's 2022 CERT-In directive requiring VPN providers to retain user data for five years.

title: Why India's censorship of news media online without stating reasons is unlawful
summary_en: Analysis of India's practice of ordering online content removal from news media without providing reasons or notification. The article argues this violates constitutional principles and examines the legal framework under the IT Act that enables secretive content takedowns.

title: India's internet shutdown crisis: A growing threat to digital rights
summary_en: Overview of India's record as the world leader in internet shutdowns, documenting the pattern of recurring shutdowns across states. Highlights how shutdowns undermine education, business, healthcare and fundamental rights, with Jammu & Kashmir and Manipur as the most affected regions.

title: Artificial Intelligence and Surveillance in India: 2025
content_snippet: A comprehensive roundup of AI-driven surveillance deployments across India in 2025, covering facial recognition systems, police use of FRT in multiple states, and the absence of legislative frameworks governing biometric surveillance.
summary_en: Comprehensive roundup of AI-driven surveillance deployments across India in 2025, covering the Maha Kumbh facial recognition system with 2,700+ AI-enhanced CCTV cameras, police use of FRT in multiple states, and the absence of any legislative framework governing biometric surveillance. Documents how India has approximately 170 facial recognition systems with a collective expenditure of around Rs 15.13 billion.

title: J&K Police Confirms Use of Face Recognition Tech in Detention of Carpenter at Pahalgam
summary_en: Jammu & Kashmir police confirmed using facial recognition technology for counter-intelligence operations, with a carpenter apprehended after being flagged by the system at the Langanbal security checkpoint near Pahalgam ahead of the Amarnath cave shrine pilgrimage. The incident raises questions about due process and potential misuse of biometric surveillance against civilians.

title: As AI Took Over Policing in Delhi, Who Bore the Brunt?
summary_en: Investigation into Delhi Police's expanding use of AI and facial recognition technology, finding that 6,630 CCTV cameras were installed across 50 police stations in 12 districts. The reporting reveals how AI-based surveillance disproportionately affects Muslim-majority areas such as North East Delhi.

title: Sanchar Saathi Reflects the Modi Government's Appetite for Surveillance
summary_en: Analysis of the Sanchar Saathi telecom security portal and how it reflects the Indian government's expanding appetite for surveillance infrastructure. The platform enables cross-referencing of telecom subscriber data with Aadhaar and other identity databases.

title: Public Infrastructure and Private Surveillance in India's Aadhaar System
summary_en: Analysis of how India's Aadhaar biometric identity system, originally built as public welfare infrastructure, has evolved into a platform for private surveillance. Examines the 2025 rules allowing private sector Aadhaar authentication and the structural privacy risks this expansion introduces.

title: Govt blocks record 28,000 URLs in 2024; Facebook, X face maximum takedowns
summary_en: The Indian government blocked a record 28,000 URLs in 2024 across Facebook, X, and other social media platforms, with Facebook and X facing the highest number of takedown orders, marking a significant escalation in government content control under the IT Act.

title: News in India is being erased from the internet
summary_en: Reports on how news stories documenting surveillance, hate crimes, and rights abuses are vanishing from Indian digital archives without explanation. The government has explicitly ordered takedowns of journalistic pieces highlighting alleged human rights abuses.

title: Mobile Internet Suspended in Jammu and Kashmir, India - 24 April 2025
summary_en: Mobile internet services were suspended in Jammu and Kashmir on April 24, 2025, amid security-related concerns and protests. The incident is part of a pattern where the region experiences recurring shutdowns, with India recording 65 internet shutdowns nationwide in 2025.

title: Indian Supreme Court's Silence on Pegasus Legitimizes Spying on Critics
source_name: Center for the Study of Organized Hate (CSOH)
summary_en: Analysis of the Indian Supreme Court's failure to act decisively on the Pegasus spyware scandal, arguing that the court's silence effectively legitimizes state surveillance of journalists, opposition politicians, and activists. Notes that over 300 Indian mobile numbers were identified as targets.

title: 'Unnecessary and disproportionate' - Experts slam 2-month VPN ban enforced in India
summary_en: Doda district in Jammu & Kashmir enforced a 2-month VPN ban starting May 2, 2025, over national security concerns. Multiple residents under 'technical surveillance' were arrested for using VPNs, with experts calling the ban 'unnecessary and disproportionate' and warning about its impact on free speech and privacy.

title: Telangana Is Inching Closer to Becoming a Total Surveillance State
summary_en: Investigation into Telangana state's expansion of CCTV and facial recognition infrastructure as part of its policing strategy. The article examines how the state is building one of India's most comprehensive surveillance networks without adequate privacy safeguards.

title: Facial Recognition in India: Part I
summary_en: Comprehensive analysis documenting approximately 170 facial recognition technology projects across India with a collective expenditure of around Rs 15.13 billion. The report highlights that all major national FRT projects (AFRS, DigiYatra, IP VSS) were implemented without any legislative framework governing their operation.

title: Ahead of India's AI Impact Summit 2026, a Report Flags Use of AI for Hate, Surveillance
summary_en: A report released ahead of India's AI Impact Summit 2026 flags the use of AI technologies for hate speech amplification and mass surveillance. The report documents the absence of concrete government plans for AI regulation despite growing deployments of facial recognition and behavioral analytics by police forces.

title: Malaysia: Social media licence plans could restrict freedom of expression
summary_en: ARTICLE 19 warned that Malaysia's new social media licensing requirement under the CMA, implemented August 2024 and enforced from January 2025, could be misused to pressure platforms into complying with government content removal requests.

title: Online safety or censorship? Malaysia's parliament passes two contentious media bills
summary_en: Malaysia's parliament passed the Communications and Multimedia (Amendment) Act 2025, expanding MCMC powers to order content removal and compel service providers to hand over user data without judicial orders.

title: Malaysia: Stop Restricting Freedom of Expression
summary_en: Amnesty International condemned the cumulative impact of Malaysia's CMA amendments, SOSMA, and Sedition Act on free expression, noting that SOSMA allows detention without judicial review for up to 28 days.

title: Malaysia: Freedom on the Net 2024
summary_en: Malaysia's internet freedom score declined to 60/100. MCMC ordered ISPs to block media outlets critical of the government and the LGBT+ app Grindr. Criminal prosecutions for social media posts criticizing religion, royalty, and the government continued.

title: Malaysia scraps loathed internet re-routing plan after public backlash
summary_en: Malaysia's MCMC attempted DNS hijacking to force all ISP traffic through government-controlled DNS servers for blanket website blocking. After massive public backlash comparing it to China's Great Firewall, the policy was abruptly reversed.

title: DNS Hijacking in Malaysia: A Step Back for Internet Freedom
summary_en: The Internet Society documented how Malaysian ISPs intercepted DNS queries to Cloudflare and Google Public DNS, redirecting them to domestic servers to enforce content blocks. The organization warned DNS hijacking is too broad and invasive.

title: Malaysia Orders Meta, TikTok to Formulate Plan for 'Harmful' Content
summary_en: Malaysia's MCMC and police summoned Meta and TikTok executives, ordering them to present plans to combat harmful content touching the '3Rs' (race, religion, royalty). The government referred 51,638 content cases in three months of 2024.

title: Sedition Act cases up 84% in 2025, says CIJ
summary_en: CIJ reported Sedition Act cases surged from 19 in 2024 to 36 in 2025 (84% increase), with 233 total investigations/arrests under speech-restricting laws. The CMA remained the most commonly invoked law.

title: MCMC sues Telegram channels over content violations, gets court order to block posts
summary_en: MCMC filed the first-ever civil action against a social media platform, suing Telegram whistleblower channels. The KL High Court granted an injunction blocking 33 specified articles. Telegram had under 50% compliance for MCMC removal requests.

title: World Report 2025: Malaysia
summary_en: HRW documented a 'rights backslide' under PM Anwar Ibrahim. The Cyber Security Act (August 2024) expanded government powers to police online expression. Social media licensing (effective January 2025) lacked clear guidelines.

title: Suaram: Sosma detentions, assembly arrests surged in 2024
summary_en: Suaram's annual report revealed SOSMA detentions surged from 66 in 2023 to 175 in 2024. Terrorism-related arrests spiked from 0 to 27, including arrests based on social media posts expressing support for terrorist groups.

title: All you need to know about Malaysia's new licence for social media platforms
summary_en: MCMC detailed the ASP(C) licensing framework requiring platforms with 8+ million Malaysian users to obtain a license by January 2025. Non-compliance carries fines up to RM500,000 or five years imprisonment.

title: Malaysia: Protect political speech and reverse decision against Telegram
summary_en: ARTICLE 19 and CIJ condemned the KL High Court injunction against two Telegram whistleblower channels, arguing that political dissent and criticism of those in power is particularly protected under international human rights standards.

title: Govt to launch Foreign Digital Identity system to curb border corruption
summary_en: Malaysia announced the Foreign Digital Identity (FDID) system requiring foreign visitors to submit fingerprints, iris scans, and facial recognition data. Immigration officers will wear body cameras with smart CCTV at all entry points.

title: Malaysia Cyber Security Act 2024 and Subsidiary Regulations in Force
summary_en: The Cyber Security Act 2024 (Act 854) came into force on August 26, 2024, granting NACSA police-equivalent investigation powers including search, seizure, and prosecution authority with only 'reasonable cause' self-determination.

title: Nigeria police charge 4 journalists with cybercrimes for corruption reporting
summary_en: Four Nigerian journalists were arrested and charged under the Cybercrimes Act for reporting on alleged banking fraud. Despite February 2024 reforms, authorities continue using cybercrime provisions to prosecute journalists.

title: Nigeria's cybercrime reforms leave journalists at risk
summary_en: February 2024 amendments to Nigeria's Cybercrimes Act still leave overly broad definitions that enable prosecution of journalists and activists, with 110 verified press attacks recorded.

title: Alleged Cyberbullying: Court grants bail to activist charged for cursing Tinubu's son
summary_en: A Lagos activist was arrested after livestreaming a video critical of President Tinubu's son. Charged with cyberbullying under the Cybercrimes Act; Federal High Court later granted bail at N10 million.

title: Nigeria's digital ID authority unveils new measures to uphold data security
summary_en: Nigeria's NIMC announced new data security measures for the NIN system after 43 million Nigerians' identity records were allegedly exposed online. Over 121 million NINs have been issued.

title: Nigeria: Freedom on the Net 2024
summary_en: Nigeria's internet freedom declined, with continued prosecution of online journalists. While the amended Cybercrimes Act was a positive step, authorities continued cracking down on protesters and activists.

title: Coalition Condemns Internet Disruptions During #EndBadGovernance Protests
summary_en: 36 organizations condemned intentional internet throttling during the August 2024 #EndBadGovernance protests. Telecom operators accused the NCC of deliberately slowing internet speeds to suppress protest information.

title: Bloody August: Nigerian Government's Violent Crackdown on #EndBadGovernance Protests
summary_en: Amnesty documented police killing at least 24 people during August 2024 #EndBadGovernance protests, with 1,200+ detained. The crackdown accompanied internet throttling to control information.

title: NIN-SIM linkage enforcement plunges Nigeria's mobile subscriptions to 218 million
summary_en: Mandatory SIM-NIN linkage enforcement led to 40 million phone lines being barred after the February 2024 deadline, with biometric identification requirements raising privacy concerns.

title: Nigeria spending billions on harmful surveillance of citizens
summary_en: Nigeria spent at least US$2.7 billion on surveillance technology (2013-2022) from China, US, Israel, UK, and EU — making it Africa's largest buyer. Technology used against activists, opposition politicians, and journalists.

title: Withdraw repressive bill to regulate bloggers or face legal action
summary_en: SERAP threatened legal action over a bill requiring social media platforms and bloggers to register local offices, calling it a 'back-door' reintroduction of the widely rejected social media bill.

title: South Africa is sleepwalking into becoming a surveillance state
summary_en: Facial recognition technology spreading through South African malls, gated estates, and public spaces with no legal oversight. POPIA's facial image protections are routinely ignored by private security firms.

title: Regulation of Digital Surveillance and the Impact on Civil Society in South Africa
summary_en: Report on how RICA Act, interception legislation, and emerging facial recognition deployments affect civil society. Identifies regulatory gaps enabling state surveillance of journalists and activists.

title: South Africa: Freedom on the Net 2024
summary_en: South Africa scored 74/100 ('Free') — the highest in the region. Disinformation spiked around 2024 elections. Government surveillance of online activity remains a concern.

title: After 13 years, SAPS can now legally use mass-surveillance cellphone grabbers
summary_en: Minister of Justice gazetted a RICA exemption allowing SAPS to purchase and deploy IMSI catchers that act as fake cellphone towers to intercept communications.

title: RICA and GILAB: Two surveillance bills that should give South Africans pause
summary_en: Analysis of RICA Amendment and GILAB bills warning that if passed, the government could mass-monitor phone conversations, emails, private messengers, and web browsing behind a veil of secrecy.

title: South Africa's surveillance law is changing but citizens' privacy is still at risk
summary_en: Despite the Constitutional Court declaring RICA unconstitutional in 2021, the proposed amendments and GILAB lack basic controls. The National Communications Centre retains bulk interception capacity without adequate safeguards.

title: The Use of Facial Recognition Technology in South Africa
summary_en: Comprehensive report on facial recognition deployments including Home Affairs' ABIS, Vumacam's camera network, and CSIR research, with analysis of racial bias and constitutional implications.

title: InfoReg slaps justice department with historic R5m fine
summary_en: The Information Regulator issued South Africa's first POPIA administrative fine — R5 million against the Department of Justice — for failing to renew cybersecurity software leading to a 2021 data breach.

title: Regulator issues enforcement notices to IEC, WhatsApp for breach of Popia
summary_en: Information Regulator issued enforcement notices to WhatsApp for applying weaker privacy protections in South Africa compared to EU users under GDPR. WhatsApp faces R10 million fine for non-compliance.

title: Vumacam captures around 4,500 suspicious vehicles and activities in Joburg daily
summary_en: Vumacam's 6,600+ cameras cover 70% of Johannesburg, detecting 4,500+ suspicious vehicles daily using AI-powered license plate recognition. Cameras are concentrated in affluent areas, bypassing townships.

title: Are South Africa's police jumping the gun on new technologies?
summary_en: ISS analysis of SAPS adopting surveillance and predictive policing technologies — CCTV, body cameras, ANPR, drones — raising concerns about poor data quality and biased outcomes in a deeply unequal country.

title: South Africa is trying to stop intelligence agency abuse — what still needs fixing
summary_en: Analysis of GILAB's passage through Parliament, splitting the State Security Agency into domestic and foreign branches. The bill still enables large-scale electronic and internet surveillance without sufficient safeguards.

title: China Orders Hotels to Scrap Facial Recognition Over Privacy Fears
summary_en: Beijing, Shanghai, Shenzhen, and Hangzhou ordered hotels to stop requiring facial recognition for check-in, responding to public concern over data privacy and deepfake risks.

title: China: Freedom on the Net 2024
summary_en: China scored 9/100 — the worst globally for the ninth consecutive year. Over 100,000 websites blocked. VPN users face heavy fines and WeChat's role intensifies self-censorship.

title: DeepSeek's AI Is Giving the World a Window into Chinese Censorship
summary_en: DeepSeek's chatbot enforces strict censorship aligned with CCP policies, refusing to answer about Tiananmen, Xi Jinping, Tibet, and Uyghur persecution.

title: How State Propaganda and Censorship Are Baked into Chinese Chatbots
summary_en: RSF tested DeepSeek, Ernie, and Qwen — all replicate Beijing's narratives, refuse sensitive topics, and dismiss Uyghur detention reports as 'baseless speculation.'

title: China Lays Down the Law on Facial Recognition
summary_en: China issued facial recognition security management measures (effective June 2025) requiring consent and banning FRT in hotel rooms and restrooms, while leaving loopholes for R&D and AI training.

title: US Tech Companies Enabled Surveillance and Detention in China
summary_en: Three-year AP investigation: IBM, Intel, NVIDIA, and Thermo Fisher sold billions in surveillance technology enabling Xinjiang mass detention targeting the Uyghur population.

title: Moscow Using Facial Recognition to Detain Men Challenging Military Conscription
summary_en: Moscow police used facial recognition in the metro to detain men who legally challenged their military conscription, flagging them as draft dodgers in government databases.

title: Russia Continues Tech Crackdown by Blocking Snapchat, FaceTime
summary_en: Roskomnadzor blocked Snapchat and FaceTime, claiming both were used for terrorism. Part of broader campaign blocking X, Facebook, and Instagram.

title: Disrupted, Throttled, and Blocked: State Censorship in Russia
summary_en: HRW report on Russian mass-scale online censorship, arbitrary shutdowns, and pressure on Apple, Google, and Mozilla to remove VPN apps since the 2022 Ukraine invasion.

title: How the Kremlin Is Using AI to Enhance Video Surveillance
summary_en: Russia expanded facial recognition from 5 to 62 regions with 1 million+ cameras (230,000 in Moscow). NtechLab developed AI for face, body, vehicle, and license plate recognition.

title: 'Lavender': The AI machine directing Israel's bombing spree in Gaza
summary_en: Investigation: Israeli army AI 'Lavender' marked 37,000 Palestinians as suspected militants. Reviewers spent ~20 seconds per target; known ~10% error rate.

title: Gaza: Israeli Military's Digital Tools Risk Civilian Harm
summary_en: HRW documented four digital tools: 'The Gospel,' 'Lavender,' 'Where's Daddy?,' and phone tracking — using faulty data risking international humanitarian law violations.

title: Iran: Freedom on the Net 2024
summary_en: Iran deployed drones, facial recognition, 'Nazer' mobile app, and IMSI catchers. National Information Network strategy for centralized digital control. Among lowest internet freedom scores globally.

title: Turkiye: Restore Access to Instagram
summary_en: Turkey blocked Instagram on August 2, 2024, after accusing Meta of removing condolence posts about Hamas leader Haniyeh's assassination. Unblocked August 10 after undisclosed agreement.

title: Joint Open Letter on Censorship in Turkiye
summary_en: HRW and civil society wrote about escalating censorship during protests following detention of Istanbul Mayor Imamoglu and 84 officials in March 2025.

title: NSO Group must pay $167M+ to WhatsApp for spyware campaign
summary_en: US jury: $167M punitive damages for exploiting a zero-day to deliver Pegasus spyware to ~1,400 users including activists and journalists.

title: Serbia: Authorities using spyware and Cellebrite tools to hack journalists
summary_en: Serbian police used Cellebrite phone-cracking tools with 'NoviSpy' spyware to infect devices of journalists and activists during detention.

title: Internet goes dark in Kenya during protests over finance bill
summary_en: Internet dropped 40% across 20+ networks during June 25, 2024 protests against the Finance Bill. NetBlocks confirmed; government denied ordering shutdown.

title: Ethiopia's internet shutdowns must not become the norm
summary_en: Condemned Ethiopia's near-year-long Amhara shutdown (Aug 2023-Jul 2024) affecting 28.9 million users, costing $1.9 billion.

title: Ethiopia: Freedom on the Net 2024
summary_en: 30 internet shutdowns over past decade, the most in Africa. State's go-to tactic for silencing dissent in Oromo and Amhara regions.

title: Myanmar enacts cybersecurity law to restrict VPN use
summary_en: Military junta criminalized unauthorized VPN use (1-6 months imprisonment). Law extends extraterritorial jurisdiction to Myanmar citizens abroad.

title: Myanmar Military Junta Enacts Repressive New Cybersecurity Bill
summary_en: Junta's Cybersecurity Law codifies overbroad censorship mandates within Myanmar's post-2021-coup digital repression context.
